summary: Gruber announces a new approach to Daring Fireball t-shirt sales, switching to periodic promotions rather than continuous orders to avoid the recurring problem of accepting orders for out-of-stock shirts. He takes personal responsibility for the fulfillment delays, absolving his partner Oddica. The post also marks the two-year anniversary of Daring Fireball as a full-time endeavor, with Gruber reflecting on how the site's revenue model has evolved from membership-driven to advertising-supplemented. He expresses pride in building a self-published, profitable website without borrowing money or relying heavily on advertising, sustained by direct reader support.
key_points: T-shirt sales are moving to periodic promotions to solve chronic out-of-stock fulfillment problems, with Gruber taking full blame for the delays
Membership revenue is no longer the majority of Daring Fireball's income but remains a significant and valued revenue stream
Gruber frames the site as a point of pride: self-published, profitable, debt-free, and built with minimal advertising
The free RSS feed going full-content reduced differentiation from member feeds, but members still get useful features like separate article and Linked List feeds
money_quotes: It is my fault, not Oddica's, that I continued to accept orders for out-of-stock shirts months in advance of the new print runs.
Membership revenue, to me, remains the purest form of support.
I'm pretty proud of what I've accomplished with Daring Fireball: a self-published, profitable web site for which I never borrowed any money, and have accepted only a modicum of advertising.
This has only been possible because of the direct support of readers like you.
My original (years ago) goal was to take this site full-time based on nothing but direct reader support; that didn't work out, but membership revenue, to me, remains the purest form of support.
key_insight: Gruber's transparency about his business model — taking blame for operational mistakes and expressing genuine gratitude for reader support — reflects the direct, accountable relationship between independent publishers and their audiences that makes self-publishing viable.
